answer: Form data can be sent to a web server for processing and storage, or used on the client-side to immediately update the interface in some way (for example, add another item to a list, or show or hide a UI feature). You would send data to a web server when you need to process or store the data remotely, and use it on the client-side when you want to update the interface immediately without server interaction.

The `<input type='email'>` field only accepts a well-formed email address and performs validation checks on the data typed by the user. On mobile devices, it causes a more appropriate keyboard layout for entering email addresses (e.g., with an @ symbol by default) to appear.

The three possible values for the `type` attribute of a `<button>` element are `submit`, `reset`, and `button`.

- A click on a `submit` button (the default value) sends the form's data to the web page defined by the `action` attribute of the `<form>` element.
- A click on a `reset` button resets all the form widgets to their default value immediately. From a UX point of view, this is considered bad practice, so you should avoid using this type of button unless you really have a good reason to include one.
- A click on a `button` button does nothing. This is useful for building custom buttons whose functionality you define with JavaScript.
Five distinct form controls are explicitly mentioned in the document: text fields, dropdown boxes, buttons, checkboxes, radio buttons.
